root_index.body:
# Redeven OKF Bundle

This top-level OKF corpus is the maintained repository knowledge surface for the current Redeven implementation. It is authored from current source code, generated contracts, and release automation rather than from removed product documents.

## Architecture

- [Runtime startup presentation](architecture/runtime-startup-presentation.md) - Structured startup events, renderer modes, and Desktop readiness reports.
- [Local UI surface](architecture/local-ui-surface.md) - Browser entrypoints, access gate, direct sessions, and Env App proxying.
- [Runtime Service snapshot](architecture/runtime-service-snapshot.md) - Desktop/runtime compatibility, open readiness, capabilities, and bindings.
- [Runtime session permission gates](architecture/runtime-session-permission-gates.md) - Runtime validation and local permission clamping before sessions open.
- [Runtime transport dependencies](architecture/runtime-transport-dependencies.md) - Flowersec and Floeterm dependency boundaries.
- [Env App upstream web dependencies](architecture/env-app-upstream-web-dependencies.md) - Published web package contracts consumed by Env App.
- [ReDevPlugin host integration boundary](architecture/redevplugin-boundary.md) - Published plugin-platform dependency and Redeven adapter ownership.
- [Plugin platform integration](architecture/plugin-platform-integration.md) - Local UI, AppServer, released ReDevPlugin Host mounting, product adapters, and Flower orchestration boundaries.
- [Container resources capability](architecture/container-resources-capability.md) - Redeven-owned Docker and Podman business capability contract for plugin adapter registration.

## Security

- [Permission policy and filesystem scope](security/permission-policy-and-filesystem-scope.md) - Local caps and directory-level file access policy.
- [Plugin platform integration security](security/plugin-platform-integration-security.md) - Session, route, permission, and capability boundaries for ReDevPlugin adapters.

## Desktop

- [Desktop runtime bridge](desktop/desktop-runtime-bridge.md) - Desktop-managed Local UI launches, runtime-control, and startup probing.
- [Desktop runtime process lifecycle](desktop/desktop-runtime-process-lifecycle.md) - Scoped inventory, historical process reconciliation, package activation ordering, and lifecycle success conditions.

## Gateway

- [Gateway service](gateway/gateway-service.md) - Standalone Gateway binary, managed service lifecycle, and Desktop bridge.

## Code

- [Browser Editor runtime](code/browser-editor-runtime.md) - Code App app server, codespace proxying, and managed Browser Editor setup.

## UI

- [UI presentation transactions](ui/ui-presentation-transactions.md) - Visual intent, after-paint content commits, post-paint effects, keep-alive continuity, and performance budgets.
- [Workbench interaction contracts](ui/workbench-interaction-contracts.md) - Wheel, text selection, and action-surface ownership contracts.
- [Plugin surfaces](ui/plugin-surfaces.md) - Redeven placement rules for sandboxed ReDevPlugin surfaces in product chrome.
- [Flower turn launcher](ui/flower-turn-launcher.md) - Contextual first-turn Ask Flower launchers and host handoff responsibilities.
- [Flower live timeline](ui/flower-live-timeline.md) - Canonical live thread timeline projection, replacement events, and cursor ownership.

## AI

- [AI tool runtime](ai/ai-tool-runtime.md) - Builtin tool registry, permission checks, and activity projection.
- [Flower plugin generation](ai/flower-plugin-generation.md) - Generated plugin flows through Floret approval and ReDevPlugin lifecycle APIs.
- [Flower context action records](ai/flower-context-action-records.md) - Ask Flower launcher context validation, persistence, and UI badge projection.
- [Redeven environment operations](ai/redeven-env-operations.md) - Product boundary for Flower and automation environment lifecycle requests.
- [OKF bundle lifecycle](ai/okf-bundle-lifecycle.md) - OKF source validation, deterministic artifacts, and runtime embedding.
- [OKF tool suite](ai/okf-search-tool.md) - Read-only repository knowledge access through index browsing, short search, and concept opening.

## Protocol

- [Gateway v1 protocol](protocol/gateway-v1-protocol.md) - OpenAPI source contract for Gateway HTTP JSON endpoints, auth, envelopes, and Desktop behavior boundaries.
- [RCPP v2 provider API](protocol/rcpp-v2-provider-api.md) - Provider discovery, Desktop auth, environment catalog, health, open-session, and bootstrap.

## Release

- [CI and release gates](release/ci-and-release-gates.md) - Local/CI checks and release artifact contracts.
- [OKF release assets](release/okf-release-assets.md) - Public release verification files for the embedded OKF bundle.

frontmatter.title: AI tool runtime

concepts.body:
Redeven's AI runtime treats tools as typed runtime capabilities. Builtins declare mutation and approval requirements, presentation metadata, grouping behavior, and renderer hints before execution dispatch reaches the runtime implementation. Floret owns the provider loop, turn execution, loop progress guards, provider-visible context lifecycle, tool dispatch lifecycle, permission/resource/approval lifecycle, streaming observation, core control-signal projection, opaque provider state lifecycle, activity observation, and engine metrics. Redeven owns product threads, persisted messages, concrete tool implementations, product policy decisions, approval UI, provider credentials, provider/profile selection, opaque provider-state persistence, and Flower timeline projection.

# Mechanism

The builtin registry includes readonly file/search/fetch helpers, standard file mutation, patch, terminal, web search, OKF index/search/open, todo, interaction, skill, and subagent tools. Flower's product permission is `permission_type`, with `readonly`, `approval_required`, and `full_access` as the only current permission values. `read_file`, `read_files`, `rgrep`, `find`, and `web_fetch` are readonly-exclusive tools for the `readonly` surface; standard shell and mutation tools are not visible there. `approval_required` and `full_access` expose the standard host tool surface instead of those readonly-exclusive helpers. `okf.index`, `okf.search`, and `okf.open` are read-only Redeven repository knowledge tools for directory discovery, short concept search, and detailed concept opening; web search is non-mutating product behavior but an open-world network effect in Floret's engine contract. `ToolPresentationSpec` is the single product display source for renderer, grouping, operation, label fields, fallback labels, compact call payload fields, result payload fields, and activity chip fields. Redeven projects builtins into Floret tool definitions with effects, read-only/destructive/open-world flags, resource extractors, static permission defaults, dynamic `PermissionFor`, and activity projection. Tool execution normalizes success summaries, errors, truncation, provenance, and builtin dispatch results so the chat activity timeline can render compact tool activity instead of raw low-level payloads; generic success summaries and event-code details remain model/audit material and are not copied into Flower expanded detail bodies by default. Flower activity detail rendering is Redeven product presentation over the Floret timeline: failures expand to a semantic `error` block that shows only the public failure reason, terminal rows expand to a read-only terminal output panel, file and patch rows render contents or diffs, web/search rows render result cards, question rows render prompts and choices, completion rows render outcome sections, todo rows render todo lists, subagent rows render delegated task summaries with message-entry actions, and generic structured rows show only allowlisted product detail fields. Flower does not fall back to raw `status`/`kind`/`tool`/`item` field tables when a renderer has no useful detail payload, and it filters non-actionable text such as `tool execution completed`, event codes, `success`, `completed`, or `ok`.

Ordinary tool scheduling follows the model's response boundaries. When one model response contains multiple ordinary tool calls, Floret v0.4.0 starts the whole batch concurrently; each call independently validates arguments, extracts resources, checks permission, waits for approval when required, dispatches, projects output, and records audit facts. Result arrays and provider transcript entries retain the model's original call order, while dispatch and result observations retain actual start and completion order. A failed, rejected, or timed-out call does not stop an independent sibling. A dependent call must be emitted by the model in a later response after the prerequisite result is visible. Neither Floret nor Redeven infers dependencies or conflicts from tool names, arguments, effects, resources, filesystem paths, shell commands, permissions, or approval requirements, and the tool registry contains no scheduling-safety metadata. Main and subagent prompts state this response-boundary contract. `MaxToolCalls=200`, context cancellation, per-call timeouts, permission and approval gates, output limits, terminal active-process capacity, and other resource limits remain protections; they are not ordering policies. Ordinary calls in a mixed response close before Floret reevaluates control signals such as `ask_user` or `task_complete`.

Floret owns the permission lifecycle. Redeven decides product policy from the current thread `permission_type` and tool metadata, then returns allow/ask/deny through the Floret `Approver` before tool dispatch. Each hosted turn installs a dynamic Floret tool surface provider: before each provider step it rebuilds the visible tools, hosted control signals, system prompt, host context, capability contract, and `PermissionSnapshot`; before local dispatch it refreshes the snapshot again so a stale model tool call cannot bypass the current thread policy. The snapshot supplies the prompt capability list, visible tool names, Floret registry names, per-tool approval decision, registry/schema/presentation hashes, and an epoch used in `tool_surface.updated` run events. In `approval_required`, shell and mutating host actions ask before running; in `full_access`, per-tool approval is skipped while schema validation, resource extraction, target routing for eligible tools, output limits, audit records, and Floret activity projection still apply. Terminal process creation, input, output access, and termination additionally require effective write and execute permission, so an execute+read runtime policy cannot become a general-purpose shell through Flower. Command classification remains useful for audit and presentation, but it is not the source of permission decisions. Already-sent provider requests cannot be rewritten retroactively, but saved thread permission changes affect the next Floret provider request and the next local tool dispatch. `subagents` is a delegation-control tool visible in all three permission types, and its own `spawn`, `send_input`, `wait`, `list`, `inspect`, `close`, and `close_all` invocations do not require approval. That allow decision covers only the delegation control action itself; child runs receive a derived dynamic tool surface from the latest parent thread permission, and child tool invocations still flow through Floret permission/resource/approval lifecycle. A child approval-required action can delegate the ask back through the parent approver path; direct child user input remains disabled. Tool handlers execute already-approved domain actions and write audit/query records such as `ai_tool_calls`, run events, and execution spans; those records support diagnostics and tool-detail lookup, not Flower UI activity generation.

Current ordinary tool approvals come from Floret's product-neutral pending approval read model. Redeven calls `ListPendingApprovals` on the active Floret host, maps each generic pending approval to a Flower `FlowerApprovalAction` using `ToolPresentationSpec`, generic effects/resources/labels/host context, permission policy, delegated approval metadata, and Floret's zero-based `batch_index` plus `batch_size`, and then publishes that product projection through Flower live state. A thread-owned queue combines main tools, delegated child tools, and control confirmations. New actions receive immutable monotonic queue order; new main actions observed in one pending snapshot are first ordered by run, step, batch index, and action id, while later asynchronous or delegated actions append in registration order. Only the queue head is `primary_action` and user-decidable; every sibling remains a read-only locator. Approving the head releases that one waiter immediately and promotes the next action while the approved handler may still be running. Rejecting or timing out one action resolves only that action. The decision timeout begins on promotion, not while queued. Stop or cancellation closes every unresolved waiter, records canceled state, and clears the queue. The thread summary status is an effective product projection: `waiting_user` has first priority, an unresolved approval queue projects `waiting_approval`, and only then do active run or persisted terminal states apply. Lightweight thread list summaries do not carry approval actions or queue details, and their omission is never an authoritative clear of selected-thread live approval state. Pending approval summaries derive their user-visible labels from the same Redeven tool presentation projection used for Floret invocation activity; Floret carries that sanitized activity presentation through approval lifecycle observations, so a requested approval is rendered as the same tool activity item with `requires_approval`, `approval_state=requested`, and `status=waiting`, not as a second approval row or a UI-layer pending approval backfill. `requires_approval` records that the invocation participated in the approval lifecycle; once Floret resolves the approval, approved pending/running/success tool rows keep that history without becoming a locked current approval row in Flower. Activity timeline rows remain audit and locator material and expose no competing Allow or Deny buttons. Submitting an ordinary approval validates queue generation/revision, head identity, expected event sequence, action revision/version, surface epoch, user/thread/run ownership, and the current Floret pending snapshot before calling Redeven's approver channel. Every successful ordinary, control-confirmation, or delegated decision returns the cursor of its persisted `approval.resolved` live event through the existing `current_cursor` response field. The shared Flower surface enters loading synchronously before transport, then uses that receipt cursor plus the current stream generation to wait for an authoritative promoted action, explicit empty queue, or terminal thread. A newer generation is treated as resync and judged from its snapshot rather than compared to the old cursor. If live projection does not reconcile within 1500 ms, the surface performs one canonical fallback reload; missing approval fields preserve the frozen card, while explicit empty approval actions, a null/zero queue, or terminal state settle it. Any stale, duplicate, already-resolved, timed-out, unavailable, or non-head decision is returned as HTTP 409 with the stable `AI_APPROVAL_CONFLICT` error code. The shared Flower surface reloads canonical state on that conflict and retries at most once only when the same action id is still current and actionable with refreshed CAS fields; if another action has been promoted or the old action has resolved, the stale decision is discarded without user-facing send-failure feedback. The task-complete confirmation surface is a Redeven `control_confirm` product confirmation for a Floret control signal, but it shares the same product queue arbitration.

Readonly-exclusive helpers are not general convenience aliases. `read_file`, `read_files`, `rgrep`, `find`, and `web_fetch` are visible only in `readonly`; `approval_required` and `full_access` use the standard host surface instead. `web_fetch` is a host-provided Redeven tool with SSRF protections, manual redirect validation, resolver and dial-time public IP checks, proxy suppression, body and MIME limits, and public-network-only semantics. DNS-derived VPN fake-IP answers in `198.18.0.0/15` are allowed for named hosts so common transparent-proxy/VPN setups can fetch public pages, but literal `198.18.0.0/15` URL hosts and other blocked local/private/reserved targets remain denied. It is not a Floret builtin and must not appear as a Floret core tool or a capability available to non-readonly permission types.

Terminal shell execution is a Redeven-owned process resource rather than a Floret-owned shell lifecycle. `terminal.exec` starts a PTY-backed process in the local AI runtime, immediately publishes a Floret `Invocation.UpdateActivity` presentation update with the public `process_id` read handle, waits only for the `yield_ms` initial wait, and returns either a completed result or a Floret pending tool result whose provider-visible handle is the same bare `process_id` used by `terminal.read`, `terminal.write`, and `terminal.terminate`. The schema also accepts `timeout_ms` as a compatibility alias for `yield_ms`; Redeven normalizes it to the same initial-wait field before persistence or activity presentation, and it is not a hard timeout or automatic process termination request. Long-running commands continue outside the provider step; `terminal.read` polls by sequence, `terminal.write` sends input to the PTY, and `terminal.terminate` cancels the process. `terminal.read` declares Floret's generic polling repeat policy and returns a progress metadata token derived from `process_id`, `last_seq`, `status`, and `ended_at_ms`, while its description tells the model to use the returned `last_seq` as the next `after_seq`. Process snapshots and terminal detail/read APIs carry status, command, cwd, output tail, sequence numbers, total bytes, duration, exit code when available, and `execution_location=local_runtime`, but the public Flower activity payload excludes host-local path/input fields such as `cwd`, `workdir`, and `stdin` as well as Floret pending lifecycle fields. Redeven caps active processes, output tails, read waits, stdin/input size, initial waits, and maximum runtime, and `Service.Close` terminates active processes instead of attempting PID recovery. A terminal process distinguishes exposed pending state, process completion, Floret settlement-in-flight, and Floret settlement acknowledgement. When a pending process finishes, Redeven asks the active run's Floret host, or a provider-free Floret `ThreadMaintenanceHost` opened over the same published store contract, to `SettlePendingTool` and applies the returned Floret projection before writing the Redeven diagnostic `ai_tool_calls` audit record or run event. Parent run cleanup waits for any in-flight terminal settlement for that run before reading a final projection; if a terminal settlement has occurred, Redeven rereads Floret `ReadTurnProjection` and applies that canonical projection instead of an older `TurnResult.Projection`. Applying a terminal settlement also publishes a canonical Flower `timeline.replaced` snapshot for the selected thread, and Redeven's projection application prevents an older pending, running, or waiting projection for the same `terminal.exec` tool item from downgrading an already terminal success, error, or canceled item. Parent run cleanup and subagent lifecycle reads also enumerate only Redeven's in-memory live terminal process manager for the matching run scope; terminal child subagents have their remaining pending processes canceled and settled through Floret public settlement APIs, while running or waiting children are left alone. Local process state is marked settlement-acknowledged only after the Floret settlement projection path succeeds; if settlement fails, later authorized lifecycle reads can retry the same public Floret settlement instead of treating audit tables as UI state. Completion does not synthesize a duplicate provider-visible tool result, does not query Floret SQLite schema directly, does not derive Flower activity from `ai_tool_calls` or run events, and does not automatically launch a follow-up model turn.

Provider adapters are model gateways, not Flower renderers. `floretProviderAdapter` maps provider stream bytes into Floret `ModelEvent` values. Assistant text, reasoning deltas, and model-generated tool-call stream observations are emitted as provider-neutral Floret model events before Redeven projects them into Flower live state. `ToolCallStream` identifies a tool call while the model is still generating it; final executable tool calls remain the separate `ToolCalls` batch. Redeven applies assistant text, reasoning deltas, tool-call stream observations, retry/done/abort markers, committed thread-entry observations, and source observations only from Floret runtime events in the event sink. Provider continuation persistence reads the complete opaque provider state from the Floret turn result, stores it as a state JSON envelope including `Attributes`, and only matches provider id, model, base URL, and state kind before passing it back to Floret.

Provider tool-call generation has a private `omit` or `enable` wire mode. `enable` serializes `parallel_tool_calls:true`; it allows a provider model to return more than one call but does not require the model to do so and is never read by the executor. Redeven enables it only for known HTTPS official OpenAI Responses/Chat, Qwen DashScope, OpenRouter, xAI, and Groq endpoints. Anthropic, DeepSeek, Moonshot, ChatGLM, Ollama, generic `openai_compatible`, Desktop opaque sources, proxies, and custom base URLs omit the field. No request path sends a false value. Hosted-turn diagnostics record the selected wire mode, and Floret lifecycle audit events carry batch index, batch size, event type, and observation time so operators can distinguish model serialization, provider single-call output, approval wait, dispatch, and completion without feeding diagnostics back into scheduling.

Thread model and reasoning defaults are Redeven product state. `ai_threads` persists `model_id` and `reasoning_selection_json`; there is no separate model-lock state. Existing thread model changes validate the requested model against the allowed runtime or Desktop model source list, require an idle mutable thread, normalize the thread reasoning selection for the new model, and clear provider continuation because the opaque continuation is keyed by provider/model/base URL/state kind. The configured `current_model_id` is only the default for the next newly created thread; it must not be synced back into existing thread defaults. When a user changes the composer model for an existing thread, Redeven updates that thread's `model_id` and then writes the same model to `current_model_id` as the future new-thread default. Turn launch resolves the effective model in this order: explicit request model, thread `model_id`, Desktop model-source override/default, then configured `current_model_id`. An explicit request model affects only that turn. User turns and waiting-input responses resolve and store their effective model and reasoning before they start or enter the queued follow-up lane, so queued work is not affected by later thread default changes. Desktop model-source descriptors carry one sanitized model capability used by model listing, thread reasoning defaults, run resolution, compaction, titles, subagents, and Floret provider controls; legacy v1 descriptors without that field are normalized from their existing context, output, modality, and reasoning fields. Floret receives the resolved model and reasoning as hosted runtime input and owns execution, provider loop, and opaque state transport only; it does not own thread preferences, provider profiles, UI policy, or product model switching.

Context pressure and compaction presentation come from structured Floret runtime observations. Redeven maps Floret context status into `context.usage.updated` and maps Floret compaction lifecycle events into `context.compaction.updated`; those product events are persisted as run events and projected into Flower live materialized state. Floret compaction debug observations are persisted separately as `floret.context.compact.debug` context run events with safe correlation, phase, token-pressure, validation, duration, provider-state-kind, error fields, and `next_action`. These debug rows are diagnostic records and never create timeline decorations or chat rows. Redeven owns only product control entrypoints, thread serialization, sanitized live projection, read-state activity, and opaque provider-state persistence for context lifecycle. Manual `/compact` requests enter Redeven only as Flower UI/RPC control signals and are forwarded into Floret `Host` compaction lifecycle APIs. Active manual compaction keeps the current Floret turn running, and `CompactionEvent(complete)` never implies a Redeven run or thread terminal state. Idle manual compaction is a Flower operation rather than an assistant run: the actor publishes the running divider and returns, a background worker calls Floret `Host.CompactThread`, and user turns submitted before the operation clears are held as queued follow-ups without transcript rows. Redeven persists only the opaque provider-state envelope returned by Floret after validating that the product thread boundary has not changed. Gate events for registered, cancel requested, commit attempted, commit rejected, and commit persisted phases make late commits, thread cancellation, and queued-turn drain diagnosable without treating diagnostics as Flower timeline rows.

Run token limits preserve distinct field semantics across the Redeven/Floret boundary. Redeven passes `RunOptions.MaxInputTokens` directly to Floret `TurnLimits.MaxInputTokens`, where it limits cumulative provider input usage across the entire hosted run. `MaxOutputTokens` remains a per-provider-request output ceiling used by Floret context policy and `ModelRequest.MaxOutputTokens`. Redeven does not add input and output limits into a derived `MaxTotalTokens`, and an unset input limit does not create an implicit cumulative token budget.

Redeven accepts a user turn only at one durable boundary. The service prepares the run object and user message payload before storage, then threadstore atomically writes the user transcript message, `ai_runs` row, `conversation_turns` anchor, thread run state, structured user inputs, secret answers, upload references, and optional source follow-up consumption. If storage fails, the reserved active run is released and no orphan transcript message is left behind. A failed terminal thread with no active run, no stop-finalizing guard, no waiting-user prompt, no idle compaction, and no queued lane is eligible for the same direct start path as an idle thread; only those active, waiting, finalizing, compacting, or queued conditions move the submitted user turn into the queue or conflict path. Conversation turns and structured user inputs remain product timeline and UI/audit records; they are not a Redeven-owned provider history source. Floret owns provider-visible history and context lifecycle through its thread store and opaque provider-state envelope. Forking a Flower thread first calls Floret's public `ForkThread` through a provider-free maintenance host, then writes the Redeven product thread fork with the returned destination `TurnID`/`RunID` references in `conversation_turns`; if the product fork write fails, Redeven compensates only through Floret public `DeleteThread`. Forked assistant/activity history stays readable through Floret `ReadTurnProjection`; Redeven does not clone Floret projection content into transcript rows.

Thread deletion is a data-lifecycle transaction, while forced cancellation remains an immediate execution-lifecycle response. Redeven can detach and cancel a stuck in-memory run without waiting for its goroutine to exit, then delete the Floret thread tree and the Redeven product thread rows. Threadstore write paths that can insert late run state verify their parent thread inside the same transaction; tool-call writes verify the parent run inside their write transaction. If deletion commits first, later run, event, execution-span, thread-state, continuation, todo, memory, tool-call, and transcript writes fail instead of recreating rows. If a write commits first, the thread deletion transaction removes it together with transcript, context, audit, permission, approval, and upload-reference state. `run.isDetached()` remains a presentation/admission signal and is not the durable deletion authority.

Flower assistant execution text and tool order come from Floret `ThreadTurnProjection`. During a live run, Floret streaming observations can update temporary markdown, while `runtime.Event.Projection` replaces the in-memory Flower assistant draft with the current Floret display projection whenever a thread entry is committed. At turn completion, Redeven maps `TurnResult.Projection` segments into Flower markdown and activity blocks. Aggregate `runtime.Event.ActivityTimeline` remains lifecycle observation data and is not a main-thread block placement source. After reload or when a terminal projection must be reconstructed without an active provider host, Redeven opens the published provider-free Floret `ThreadMaintenanceHost` and calls `ReadTurnProjection` with explicit `ThreadID`, `TurnID`, and `RunID`. Floret live event projections, `TurnResult.Projection`, `ThreadMaintenanceHost.ReadTurnProjection`, and pending settlement projections are the authoritative raw-capable current-turn display projections; default preview detail reads are not a source for final assistant markdown. A committed projection is applied atomically: if any Floret activity segment fails the public timeline validator, Redeven records the diagnostic and rejects that projection instead of replacing an existing live activity row with an empty markdown block. Within a single run, a terminal Floret projection for a `terminal.exec` item is a lifecycle floor: later older pending/running/waiting projections for the same tool item cannot overwrite its success/error/canceled terminal state. A completed, failed, or canceled hosted turn must not use `TurnResult.Output`, task-complete signal text, provider deltas, preview detail rows, aggregate activity timelines, or audit rows as a fallback execution transcript; those values may drive lifecycle, control, telemetry, or thread-list preview state, but assistant markdown and activity block order in Flower responses must remain derived from Floret's public projection. Redeven no longer stores Floret-backed assistant projection JSON as `transcript_messages` shadow rows; `transcript_messages` remains Redeven-owned user/product message storage, while `conversation_turns` records only product message and Floret turn correlation. Thread `last_message_preview` is Redeven-owned thread-list summary state and may be updated from the final assistant projection text without becoming a transcript row. Flower UI tool activity comes from Floret `ThreadTurnProjection` activity segments whose `ActivityTimeline` presentation was produced from Redeven `ToolPresentationSpec` before crossing into Floret. The expanded detail body is a renderer-first product projection in `flowerActivityPresentation`: it consumes only the timeline item renderer, label, chips, public payload, file action metadata, and subagent public route facts, and it does not query `ai_tool_calls`, audit tables, threadstore rows, or Floret SQLite to fill fields. A running terminal detail may use the timeline payload's public `process_id` to call Redeven's terminal read API for live output; that read path is detail presentation only and never creates activity rows, transcript messages, or lifecycle facts. Floret's local `tool_call` activity means the model requested a queued tool invocation; only Floret's `tool_dispatch_started` lifecycle fact marks that same item running after permission and approval gates pass. Batched sibling calls therefore remain visible as pending instead of running while an earlier sibling waits for approval. Redeven can key detail lookups from timeline item ids, but it must not synthesize chat activity rows from `ai_tool_calls`, `tool.call`, `tool.result`, execution spans, handler outcomes, provider-adapter side effects, pending approval snapshots, aggregate live activity timelines, or stale live activity leftovers.

Floret v0.4.0 distinguishes a terminal engine result from a terminal projection read failure with `ErrTurnProjectionUnavailable`. Redeven retries `ReadTurnProjection` once with its independent persistence timeout and accepts the result only when nonempty `ThreadID`, `TurnID`, and `RunID` exactly match the active run. A correctly identified projection with zero segments remains a canonical empty projection and can clear placeholders. If the retry fails, Redeven keeps already streamed blocks, does not apply an empty projection, and marks the turn failed with `floret_projection_unavailable`; the user is told to refresh the thread and not to rerun a task that may already have produced side effects. When Redeven preserves a terminal `terminal.exec` item over a stale active item, it calls Floret `RebuildActivitySummary` for counts, severity, attention, approval count, duration, and run-level error/canceled status instead of maintaining a second summary reducer.

Flower subagents use the published Floret `Host` lifecycle rather than a Redeven-owned goroutine task manager. Main Flower runs, subagents, and compaction share the Floret-managed `stateDir/ai/floret_threads.sqlite` store, and Redeven accesses that store only through Floret public runtime APIs. Redeven caches one Floret subagent runtime per parent Flower thread so child controllers and the Floret host can outlive an individual parent turn, while restart recovery uses provider-free `ThreadMaintenanceHost` over the same published store contract without querying SQLite directly. Parent host initialization uses Floret `EnsureThread` instead of `ReadThread`. Parent Stop, CancelThread, and CancelRun are execution-lifecycle operations: after canceling the parent run they call Floret `CloseSubAgents` for the parent thread, which closes unfinished descendants, clears queued child input, and preserves completed history. A top-level parent hosted turn that exits failed, canceled, timed out, or with a Floret run error is also an execution-lifecycle terminal boundary: before Redeven performs product terminal cleanup, it classifies the parent reason (`parent_failed`, `parent_cancelled`, or `parent_timed_out`), calls Floret public `CloseSubAgents` through the active child host, parent host, or provider-free maintenance host, and republishes the resulting canonical child snapshots through `thread.subagents`. DeleteThread is a data-lifecycle operation: Redeven first stops active execution, then calls Floret `DeleteThread` for the parent engine thread so Floret cascades deletion through descendants, prompt scopes, and artifacts; only after that does Redeven delete Flower product projections, uploads, and audit/detail records. `Service.Close` releases local host resources during process shutdown. The `subagents` builtin validates `spawn`, `send_input`, `wait`, `list`, `inspect`, `close`, and `close_all`, then forwards lifecycle operations to Floret `SpawnSubAgent`, `SendSubAgentInput`, `WaitSubAgents`, `ListSubAgents`, `CloseSubAgent`, and `CloseSubAgents`; parent child-list refresh calls Floret `ListSubAgents` through the active host or `ThreadMaintenanceHost.ListSubAgents` and publishes `thread.subagents` patches instead of creating independent main-thread activity blocks. `wait` defaults to five minutes and caps requested waits at twenty minutes, while child runs pass a twenty-minute Floret subagent run timeout. The child `ThreadID` is the canonical subagent identity and is also returned as `subagent_id` for model-facing compatibility. Redeven stores a distinct product `child_run_id` for child permission snapshots and delegated approval refs; Floret execution `RunID` remains a per-engine-execution identity and is not interpreted as the product child run id. Delegated approval refs reject missing child run ids, child-thread aliases, and parent-run aliases. `spawn` persists a provisional child permission snapshot keyed by the real Floret `subagents` tool call id, calls `SpawnSubAgent`, and finalizes the snapshot only after host spawn succeeds; those stored snapshots remain product audit and permission records rather than child transcript ownership. Active child hosts install their own dynamic tool surface provider, derive the child surface from the latest parent thread permission on the next provider step and next tool dispatch, preserve child thread id and product `child_run_id` in host context, and emit `subagent.tool_surface.updated` when the child epoch changes. Task names and paths are labels, not lifecycle targets, and the model cannot supply a child `thread_id` or raw `fork_mode`. The parent model chooses `context_mode` on `spawn`: `mission_only` maps to `SubAgentForkNone` and `full_history` maps to `SubAgentForkFullPath`; after spawn, wait/list/detail payloads and parent activity presentation derive `context_mode` only from Floret `ForkMode` instead of a Redeven memory map or duplicate persistence. The thread-scoped child host registers the parent thread's delegated tool surface for the current permission snapshot, removes parent-only controls, and disables hidden Floret control surfaces. If the parent run's model/tool/session configuration changes, the runtime rebuilds the child host only when no child is active; active children continue on the existing host generation while still refreshing permission-derived tool surfaces. Child tools use the Floret invocation `ThreadID` and `TurnID` to verify execution scope, while Redeven audit records and execution spans use the explicit product `child_run_id` from host context rather than deriving it from Floret execution `RunID`. Child threads run with a Flower child prompt that forbids direct user input, states the visible tool list and context mode, and requires a complete but focused final handoff report.

Delegated approval is thread-owned product state, not subagent detail state. Redeven stores delegated approval requests with parent thread, parent user, parent run/turn, child thread/run/turn, child tool call, approval id, ref hash, request fingerprint, delivery state, version, and surface epoch. User decisions submit through the parent thread with a delegated ref; the store compares owner, ref hash, action id, version, surface epoch, and idempotency key before resolving. Idempotent replay is accepted only when the key matches the same action, same ref hash, and same decision. Resolved delivery uses compare-and-swap; if delivery acknowledgement cannot be confirmed, Redeven marks the record as acknowledgement-unknown instead of emitting a false delivered state. The parent Flower waiting projection is the only approval surface; subagent detail is a read-only mirror or locator.

Subagent data is intentionally split into model-facing summaries, parent timeline presentation, thread-level child state, and human detail. `subagents(action="spawn")` requires a short `task_name`, a user-facing one-sentence `task_description`, an `agent_type`, and the full delegated `message`; the task description is the stable responsibility text shown in parent Flower UI, while live progress belongs in the child detail overlay. Model-facing `wait` results return bounded status `items` with child identity, task name, task description, agent type, status, timing, timeout facts, and `detail_ref`; they do not include child transcripts, tool calls, raw output, commands, stdout, stderr, `last_message`, `waiting_prompt`, or `result_digest`. Completed waits can include `final_handoff_report`, which carries each child’s final handoff text under a token budget without process detail. Timed-out waits can include `progress_summary`, which carries current status, waiting blockers when relevant, and suggested parent actions instead of execution logs. List and inspect remain bounded monitoring views with detail refs. Parent Flower timeline rows come from Floret `ThreadTurnProjection` activity segments and are projected to a public subagent payload before Flower renders them. That payload keeps only route/display facts such as `thread_id`, `subagent_id`, `parent_thread_id`, `action`, `status`, `task_name`, `title`, `task_description`, `agent_type`, canonical `items`, item status/timing, `timed_out`, `truncated`, counts, and public `error` or summary fields; it filters raw child content, private paths, detail refs, context/debug diagnostics, `last_message`, `waiting_prompt`, queued input counts, `can_*` control flags, and fields outside the current subagent payload allowlist. Flower activity rows render action-specific subagent titles, task/name previews, task descriptions, running elapsed time when available, exceptional status only when the child failed, canceled, timed out, or needs input, and an `Open messages` action derived only from the public `thread_id`/`subagent_id` payload fields.

The parent thread header Sub Agents dropdown is not a timeline reconstruction. Redeven lists child membership through Floret `ListSubAgents` on the active host, or through provider-free `ThreadMaintenanceHost.ListSubAgents` after runtime restart, maps that product-neutral state into `thread.subagents`, includes it in live bootstrap, and publishes the same list in `thread.patched` when subagent lifecycle events arrive. Flower builds the header badge and dropdown only from `thread.subagents`; it does not scan transcript blocks, `ActivityTimeline` rows, audit records, hidden sidecars, or thread list summaries. If a thread list refresh summary omits `subagents`, that omission preserves selected-thread detail state; only a bootstrap or live patch that explicitly owns `subagents` can replace or clear the list. Redeven does not query Floret SQLite tables, write duplicate child membership into Redeven DB tables, or create child Flower metadata as a display source. The overlay reads `GET /api/ai/threads/{parent}/subagents/{child}/detail`, which validates the parent thread in Redeven, uses the active Floret host when present or a provider-free `ThreadMaintenanceHost` otherwise, calls Floret `ReadSubAgentDetail` through a parent-scoped public contract, maps Floret child not-found or ownership mismatch through `errors.Is` against Floret public runtime not-found sentinels to 404, and renders Floret's top-level canonical child activity timeline together with user/assistant messages, tool results, approvals, turn markers, compactions, errors, and custom rows from the child execution timeline. Preview-mode subagent detail is bounded inspection data and must not be used as the complete human transcript for child user or assistant messages; a complete human detail surface requires an explicit Floret display-detail contract while model-facing `wait`, `list`, and `inspect` results remain bounded. Event-row activity snapshots are not a display source, so later tool results or pending-tool settlements replace earlier running updates in the same canonical activity block. Child detail does not require a child row or child Flower metadata in Redeven threadstore, and subagents do not appear as child threads in the sidebar. Subagent model results use canonical `items` only; unsupported detail, transcript, raw-output, or non-allowlisted collection fields are filtered before they reach model-facing results or Flower activity payloads.

Control signal projection is separate from Redeven waiting UI persistence. Floret signal projection emits engine signal facts for `ask_user` and `task_complete`; Flower `RequestUserInputPrompt` objects are created only by Redeven's waiting projection and persistence layer.

`terminal.exec` is the local AI runtime shell. Its schema describes local PTY process execution, including `yield_ms` for the initial wait and `timeout_ms` only as a compatibility alias for that same wait. Terminal results include `execution_location=local_runtime` plus the resolved local working directory and process identity. Thread target context, Welcome environment context, or a target-shaped id does not change where this builtin runs. Remote or target diagnostics must use Redeven product commands whose own result payload carries target provenance.

When a thread is configured for explicit target routing, the runtime forwards target-scoped builtin calls through `TargetToolExecutor`. The target executor receives a `TargetToolCall` containing `target_id`, `tool_name`, sanitized arguments, and required capabilities. The run layer returns a result payload that preserves or injects `target_id` and `execution_location`, so target-routed tool results cannot lose provenance before they reach the model or activity timeline.

Flower execution uses the published Floret runtime package through `runFloretHostedTurn` and `flruntime.Host`. Redeven passes the current user input, current-turn supplemental context, initial tool registry, dynamic tool surface provider, control signals, provider adapter, opaque previous provider state, and loop limits to Floret; it does not pass a preassembled provider-visible history. Supplemental context is the only path for accepted Ask Flower linked context to become model-visible in the current turn: file items are metadata-only, attachments become `attachment_metadata`, monitoring items are process metadata, terminal items carry short selections or length-only truncation metadata, and Git/environment snapshots carry bounded generated summaries. Each accepted context-action item must map to exactly one supplemental item; formatter failure aborts before provider invocation. Floret `Metrics.Steps` is consumed as telemetry for result projection and activity accounting, not as a product configuration or request field. `ask_user` and `task_complete` use Floret core control definitions and provider-safe projection. Ordered assistant/tool display is derived from Floret `ThreadTurnProjection` segments, while the shape of Flower markdown blocks, activity timeline blocks, file actions, and tool presentation remains Redeven product projection.

# Boundaries

Tool names are not aliases for deleted knowledge-era tools. Current repository knowledge access uses `okf.index`, `okf.search`, and `okf.open`: index for broad directory discovery, search for short candidate lists, and open for detailed concept content. OKF is an embedded project corpus and does not access the internet; external, current, recent, news, third-party, market, pricing, and general web facts must use direct authoritative URLs or web search discovery instead.

Target provenance is part of the tool contract, not a UI hint. Flower must not infer remote execution from thread context alone; it can only claim remote or target execution when a tool result or Redeven product command returns explicit execution provenance.

Redeven must consume published Floret releases. Repository builds, tests, Desktop runs, and release validation must not depend on a sibling checkout, Go workspace, local replace directive, or package-manager local link. The runtime boundary depends on the published `github.com/floegence/floret` release that exposes Floret's `Host` turn and compaction facade, `RunTurnRequest.SupplementalContext`, `TurnSupplementalContextItem`, `ThreadTurnProjection`, live `runtime.Event.Projection`, transcript-free `EnsureThread`, dynamic tool surface provider hook, explicit execution `RunID` turn contracts, aggregate `runtime.Event.ActivityTimeline` lifecycle observations, `Invocation.UpdateActivity` / `tool_activity_updated` running presentation updates, committed thread-entry observations whose nonterminal turn markers do not settle tool activity, queued local tool calls that become running only at `tool_dispatch_started`, structured context observations, cancellation-aware manual compaction lifecycle events, cancellation-preserving turn-result projections after a host stops a run, manual compaction admission no-op observations for short or low-savings context, opaque provider state, provider request budget validation, multi-tool batch boundary preservation during compaction, continuation transcript delta preservation, Floret-owned activity timeline detail and subagent activity timeline projections, tool approval activity as a lifecycle state on the tool item until explicit resolution, structured tool-result status, parent-scoped subagent detail API, product-neutral pending approval snapshots, detail-only pending tool settlement, `CloseSubAgents`, `ForkThread`, durable `ForkMode`, and cascading engine thread deletion. Redeven must not import Floret `internal/` packages or depend on provider-visible transcript, summary, range-selection, checkpoint payload, engine policy data, or raw tool arguments for Flower approval presentation.

concepts.snippet: Redeven's AI runtime treats tools as typed runtime capabilities. Builtins declare mutation and approval requirements, presentation metadata, grouping behavior, and renderer hints before execution dispatch reaches the runtime implementation.

concepts.title: Flower context action records
concepts.description: Ask Flower context actions are validated product records that become current-turn Floret supplemental context without becoming Redeven-owned provider history.

frontmatter.description: Ask Flower context actions are validated product records that become current-turn Floret supplemental context without becoming Redeven-owned provider history.
frontmatter.title: Flower context action records

concepts.body:
Redeven treats user-selected Ask Flower context as a first-class product input record. The record is persisted with the user turn, used for Flower UI badges and product routing visibility, and projected into Floret as current-turn supplemental context. It is not copied into Redeven-owned provider history, long-term memory, permission state, or runtime authority.

# Mechanism

Clients send a standard `assistant.ask.flower` context action with the turn input. The AI service validates the action against the Flower context-action contract and normalizes it before accepting the user turn. Validation includes a source/kind privacy matrix: file browser, file preview, and editor preview accept only `file_path`; terminal accepts only `terminal_selection`; monitoring accepts only `process_snapshot`; Git browser and Desktop Welcome environment cards accept only generated `text_snapshot`. File and process context items reject hidden text payload fields, and malformed or non-Flower action records fail instead of silently becoming context-free turns. Empty context is invalid. Terminal character counts use Unicode code points, process usage values must be finite and nonnegative, and process identity, platform, and capture time are required.

The backend writes schema v2 context items through a kind-dispatched canonical JSON writer. Required zero-value fields remain present: files always include `is_directory`, terminal items always include `selection` and `selection_chars`, process items always include CPU and memory, and text snapshots always include `content`. The same writer is used for transcript messages and queued-turn persistence, so immediate, queued, and restart-restored turns have one wire shape. Unknown item kinds fail before persistence. The accepted context action remains attached to the product transcript message and can be projected by Flower as compact linked context. Structured response records, secret answers, and upload bindings are persisted in the same turn-start flow so product UI and audit reads have one durable boundary.

Flower execution passes the current user input to Floret through the published `Host` facade from `github.com/floegence/floret` v0.3.89 or newer. Immediately before `Host.RunTurn`, Redeven formats the normalized Ask Flower record and attachment list into Floret `SupplementalContext` items. Every accepted context item must produce exactly one supplemental item; formatting errors stop the turn before provider invocation instead of degrading to a context-free request. Process snapshots include process metadata such as PID, name, username, CPU, memory, platform, and capture time. File context items are metadata-only and carry path, directory flag, root label, source surface, target, and suggested working directory. Short terminal selections may carry the selected text; long terminal selections become metadata-only length/truncation records. Git and environment snapshots may carry bounded product-generated summaries. Normal attachments become `attachment_metadata` items with non-dereferenceable labels such as name and MIME type; upload URLs and file contents are not copied into Floret input or supplemental context. Redeven emits `flower.context_action.injected` with aggregate fields only: schema/action/provider/source/target, supplemental item count, rendered character count, truncation state, attachment item count, and a context hash.

Shared Flower UI parsing has two explicit modes. `strict-input` is used by Env App and Desktop adapters and accepts only canonical new-turn records. `persisted-display` is used for transcript history and restores only deterministic schema v2 omissions caused by the former Go `omitempty` writer: missing file `is_directory` becomes false, missing empty terminal selection/count becomes empty/zero, missing process CPU/memory becomes zero, and missing text content becomes empty display text. Historical `file_selection` can be displayed but cannot be submitted, queued, or projected into Floret. A damaged or future individual item in an otherwise valid envelope becomes a noninteractive unsupported-context chip without exposing its raw payload; invalid envelope identity or a known source/kind mismatch rejects the whole display record.

Persisted display and host navigation have separate authority. A canonical `file_path` becomes an interactive transcript chip only when the current host exposes the matching file-preview or directory-browser capability. Env App normalizes that path and sends it through its existing filesystem preview or listing surface, where normal session and filesystem read policy remains authoritative. Desktop Welcome exposes no such capability, so the same record is display-only there. The host read happens only after a user click and never writes file contents, preview output, upload URLs, or file selections back into the context action, queued turn, Floret input, supplemental context, or context-action events. Self-contained text and process records may use Flower's local snapshot preview, but that preview does not resolve or read live files.

# Boundaries

User-provided context is not a permission grant, not a working-directory mutation, not long-term memory, and not a Redeven-controlled provider-history segment. Floret owns provider-visible history, compaction, opaque provider continuation, and durable context lifecycle; Redeven only supplies host-provided supplemental context for the current user turn. `suggested_working_dir_abs` is product context only; runtime authority still comes from session metadata and existing thread/runtime execution policy. Execution context fields guide target selection, but they do not authorize direct Docker, SSH, systemd, launchctl, or process-manager operations. Persisted transcript `contextAction` remains an observable record, but it is not reused as model context outside the turn being launched. Queued-turn detail exposes only prompt, message id, creation time, and canonical context action; attachment transport URLs remain outside that DTO. The canonical transcript message uses the same client message id and replaces the pending projection without duplicate context chips. Direct API callers, queued turns, and persisted user messages must use the same strict Flower context-action validator; malformed or non-Flower actions fail instead of being dropped into a context-free turn.

[3] redeven:internal/ai/send_user_turn.go:338 - Structured user input context is persisted as product state attached to the response message.
[4] redeven:internal/ai/context_action.go:234 - Runtime enforces nonempty context and the source/kind privacy matrix.
[5] redeven:internal/ai/context_action_floret.go:27 - Redeven projects normalized Ask Flower context and attachments into Floret supplemental context items.
[6] redeven:internal/ai/context_action_floret.go:83 - Attachments are projected as metadata-only supplemental context items.

concepts.title: Flower plugin generation
concepts.description: Flower-generated plugins are Redeven product orchestration through Floret and released ReDevPlugin lifecycle APIs.

frontmatter.description: Flower-generated plugins are Redeven product orchestration through Floret and released ReDevPlugin lifecycle APIs.
frontmatter.title: Flower plugin generation

concepts.body:
Flower can help users create plugins, but the generated-plugin flow is not a
second plugin platform. Redeven owns the user intent capture, prompt
orchestration, review UX, approval presentation, and product timeline
projection. ReDevPlugin owns templates, validators, package builders, lifecycle
APIs, runtime contracts, sandbox surfaces, and platform policy. Floret owns the
tool dispatch and approval lifecycle that Flower must use.

# Mechanism

A Flower-generated plugin flow is a sequence of approved product actions over
released ReDevPlugin APIs. Flower may draft source and manifest content, call
released scaffold, validate, package, install, enable, open, diagnostics,
export/import, update, or uninstall APIs, and show the user review states. The
actual package validity, trust model, lifecycle state, sandbox bootstrap,
permissions, storage/network/runtime access, and operation details remain
ReDevPlugin facts.

Flower tool handlers execute only after Floret permission, resource extraction,
and approval gates allow the domain action. A plugin-generation handler may run
already-approved local steps and call released ReDevPlugin adapters, but it
must not perform its own approval wait, bypass Floret dispatch, mutate Flower
activity rows directly, or convert plugin audit rows into Flower timeline
state. Flower activity must come from Floret `ActivityTimeline` /
`ThreadTurnProjection` and Redeven `ToolPresentationSpec`; plugin operations
and build diagnostics are detail lookup material keyed from approved activity or
operation ids.

Prompt-first generation can propose source, manifest shape, capability intent,
and test plans, but validation is structural. Redeven should use prompt and
contract guidance for intent and complexity, then rely on released ReDevPlugin
validators, package builders, generated SDKs, and compatibility hashes for
platform correctness. If a generated plugin needs a platform capability that
does not exist in released ReDevPlugin, the requirement is recorded upstream
instead of hidden behind a Redeven-local helper.

# Boundaries

Flower must not write plugin registry rows, mint bridge tokens, place plugin UI
assets directly under Env App routes, bypass package signatures or trust
policy, grant storage/network/runtime access outside ReDevPlugin brokers, or
call a business adapter before ReDevPlugin has built the request context.

Flower must not create a Redeven-only manifest format, permissive validator,
package builder, WASM ABI, asset-ticket scheme, runtime supervisor, storage
broker, network broker, or generated SDK shape. Build and package actions are
ordinary Floret-approved Redeven tools over released ReDevPlugin commands or
libraries, not arbitrary shell shortcuts that become a hidden platform surface.

Pending approvals for generated-plugin actions come from Floret's current
pending approval model and Redeven's product projection, not from historical
plugin install audit, ReDevPlugin confirmation rows, or guessed UI state. A
requested approval remains waiting until Floret resolves it.

# Citations

[1] redeven:AGENTS.md:204 - AI behavior should be prompt-first and contract guided.
[2] redeven:AGENTS.md:319 - Redeven Flower/Floret orchestration calls released ReDevPlugin lifecycle APIs.
[3] redeven:AGENTS.md:368 - Flower/Floret tools must not bypass Floret approval or ReDevPlugin policy.
[4] redeven:AGENTS.md:487 - Flower-generated plugin flows are Redeven orchestration over ReDevPlugin primitives.
[5] redeven:AGENTS.md:554 - Flower may generate source and drive lifecycle through released ReDevPlugin APIs only.
[6] redeven:AGENTS.md:588 - Redeven code must not bypass Floret lifecycles.
[7] redeven:AGENTS.md:589 - Tool approval flows through Floret `PermissionSpec`, resource extraction, and `Approver`.
[8] redeven:AGENTS.md:604 - Redeven audit/query records are detail sources, not Flower UI activity generation.
[9] redeven:AGENTS.md:608 - `ToolPresentationSpec` is the single product display source for activity presentation.
[10] redeven:okf/ai/ai-tool-runtime.md:15 - Floret owns permission lifecycle and Redeven returns allow, ask, or deny before dispatch.

concepts.title: OKF bundle lifecycle
concepts.description: Redeven builds a deterministic embedded OKF bundle from source Markdown concepts.

frontmatter.description: Redeven builds a deterministic embedded OKF bundle from source Markdown concepts.
frontmatter.title: OKF bundle lifecycle

concepts.body:
Redeven builds its embedded OKF bundle from curated source files under the top-level `okf/` authoring root, verifies deterministic dist outputs under `okf/dist/`, and embeds the resulting bundle into the binary used by runtime and AI features.

# Mechanism

`build_assets.sh` invokes `build_okf_bundle.sh`, which runs `cmd/okf-bundle` against explicit source and dist roots. The builder scans OKF Markdown concepts, validates required frontmatter and reserved filenames, skips generated `dist/` while hashing the source tree into `source_sha256`, writes bundle plus manifest plus sha outputs, CI validates source integrity and stale dist, and top-level `go:embed` makes the bundle available to runtime search code.

# Boundaries

The bundle remains auditable only while OKF Markdown concepts are the sole authoring surface and `okf/dist/*` stays a generated verification artifact set rather than hand-edited truth.

# Citations

concepts.title: OKF tool suite
concepts.description: OKF tools expose read-only Redeven repository knowledge through index browsing, short search, and concept opening.

frontmatter.description: OKF tools expose read-only Redeven repository knowledge through index browsing, short search, and concept opening.
frontmatter.title: OKF tool suite

concepts.body:
Redeven exposes the embedded OKF bundle through read-only `okf.index`, `okf.search`, and `okf.open` builtin tools, and Env App renders OKF activity as project knowledge lookup inside Flower's compact activity timeline.

# Mechanism

Runtime OKF code loads the embedded bundle once, indexes root directory sections, scores concepts in memory, and opens concept bodies by id or path. The bundle builder parses internal Markdown links into deterministic `links` and `backlinks` so concept detail can expose graph context without scanning source Markdown at runtime. Builtin tool registration advertises OKF tools as non-mutating local-read tools with structured presentation metadata, tool execution dispatches behind a read-permission gate, and `ActivityTimelineBlock` renders compact knowledge-lookup activity with detail access for directory sections, concept matches, opened concept metadata, and link graph payloads. OKF activity labels and operation values come from `ToolPresentationSpec`, so the Floret projection does not own a separate OKF display vocabulary.

# Tool workflow

`okf.index` browses `okf/index.md` as a structured directory and should be used first for broad Redeven-internal questions when the relevant concept area is unclear. `okf.search` returns a bounded short candidate list with `concept_id`, `path`, title, description, resource, tags, snippet, score, returned count, total match count, and explicit `has_more` metadata; broad searches should start with the default small result count. Limiting search to a short candidate list is progressive disclosure, not content truncation. `okf.open` opens one concept by `concept_id` or path and returns metadata, a body window, and links/backlinks. Models should open the relevant concept before relying on OKF for detailed facts, boundaries, contracts, or workflows.

# Boundaries

The tools return scoped Redeven repository knowledge and do not mutate OKF source or dist artifacts. They do not access the internet and are not a substitute for web search, direct URL fetching, or external source validation. They must not be used for current news, recent public events, market or pricing facts, third-party documentation, external facts, or general web facts. Source-level conclusions still require file or terminal verification after OKF navigation.

# Citations

[1] redeven:internal/okf/search.go:17 - LoadEmbeddedBundle reads the embedded bundle lazily once.
[2] redeven:internal/okf/search.go:37 - `Index`, `Search`, and `Open` load the embedded bundle and return structured OKF payloads.
[3] redeven:internal/okf/search.go:78 - Search caps max results and filters optional type and tag constraints.
[4] redeven:internal/ai/tools/registry.go:249 - Builtin presentation metadata keeps OKF tools read-only with structured rendering.
[5] redeven:internal/ai/builtin_tool_handlers.go:662 - Tool definition text limits OKF tools to Redeven repository knowledge and excludes internet/current/general web facts.
[6] redeven:internal/ai/builtin_tool_handlers.go:33 - OKF success summaries use knowledge-lookup wording rather than the raw tool name.
[7] redeven:internal/ai/run.go:3186 - Tool execution dispatches OKF builtins to index, search, and open runtime APIs.
[8] redeven:internal/okf/parser.go:154 - The bundle builder parses internal Markdown links for OKF graph context.
[9] redeven:internal/ai/floret_tools.go:716 - Floret result activity labels are derived from tool presentation label fields and fallbacks.

concepts.title: Redeven environment operations
concepts.description: redeven env and redeven targets exec are the product boundaries for Flower and automation environment lifecycle and target diagnostic requests.

frontmatter.description: redeven env and redeven targets exec are the product boundaries for Flower and automation environment lifecycle and target diagnostic requests.
frontmatter.title: Redeven environment operations

concepts.body:
Redeven environment lifecycle requests must go through the Redeven product surface, not through inferred container, SSH, systemd, launchctl, or process-manager commands. Flower learns this boundary through the standard `redeven-environment` system skill, not through a bespoke environment workflow embedded in the global prompt.

# Mechanism

The `redeven env` CLI surface exposes machine-readable environment status, diagnostics, and lifecycle operation plans through the shared agent protocol envelope. `list`, `resolve`, `status`, `diagnose`, and `stop` are executable for the supported default Local Environment path. `start`, `restart`, and `update` are phase-one planning entries: they return structured availability, reason codes, and next actions rather than pretending the CLI can start or update Desktop runtime sessions. When the target catalog contains SSH, container, provider, Gateway, or external Local UI entries, `redeven env` may resolve the target but still returns the lifecycle request as `supported=false` with `reason_code=unsupported_target_kind` unless the target is the default Local Environment.

Ask Flower environment context supplies routing data through the standard context action. When the accepted turn starts, Redeven can project these fields into Floret current-turn supplemental context metadata, but they remain product context and UI/audit facts rather than permission, remote execution proof, builtin target routing, or Redeven-owned provider history. The `redeven-environment` skill tells Flower to choose targets in this order: `execution_context.current_target_id`, then `target.target_id`, then `execution_context.source_env_public_id`, then `current`. The literal target `current` is accepted by Redeven target resolution as the current default environment alias.

For arbitrary target OS diagnostics, Flower should use `redeven targets exec --target <target> --command <agent-selected command> --json`. This command lets the agent choose OS-appropriate probes such as `date`, `uname`, uptime, disk, process, package manager, or service checks while Redeven owns target resolution, execution location, timeout, stdout/stderr, and exit-code provenance. The command supports the default Local Environment shell and saved local/SSH host runtime targets with key or agent SSH auth. Password SSH targets, container placements, provider environments, Gateway environments, and external Local UI targets return structured unsupported or unavailable results instead of falling back to ad hoc low-level commands.

# Boundaries

`redeven env` and `redeven targets exec` output is sanitized for Flower and automation. Target and runtime summaries omit runtime-control tokens and avoid leaking local state, config, socket, and agent home paths where those paths are not needed for the contract. Diagnostic output may include local state, lock, and socket paths when those paths are necessary to explain runtime attach state. Operation commands in the environment JSON contract may name Redeven product commands only. Target execution JSON must include `target_id` and `execution_location` when execution occurs. If an operation or target execution returns a structured unavailable, unsupported, or blocked result, Flower should explain that result and the product next action instead of inventing a lower-level workaround.

`terminal.exec` remains the local AI runtime shell. It starts an interactive PTY process with a local `process_id`, can be inspected with `terminal.read`, driven with `terminal.write`, and stopped with `terminal.terminate`. It can be used to invoke Redeven product commands, but it must not be described as remote execution unless the product command result itself contains target execution provenance such as `execution_location=ssh_target`.

# Citations

[1] redeven:cmd/redeven/env.go:13 - The `env` command dispatches lifecycle subcommands.
[2] redeven:internal/agentprotocol/env.go:60 - Environment status, runtime summary, diagnostics, and operation plans define the JSON contract.
[3] redeven:internal/agentprotocol/env.go:120 - Environment target resolution distinguishes supported local targets from recognized unsupported target shapes.
[4] redeven:internal/agentprotocol/targets.go:37 - Target discovery projects the default local target and saved catalog connections into target descriptors.
[5] redeven:internal/agentprotocol/target_exec.go:39 - Target command execution returns structured target, command, location, stdout/stderr, exit-code, timeout, and support fields.
[6] redeven:internal/ai/system_skills/redeven-environment/SKILL.md:1 - The system skill teaches Flower the Redeven environment and target execution command surfaces.
[7] redeven:internal/ai/context_action_floret.go:200 - Ask Flower execution metadata is projected into Floret supplemental context metadata for the current turn.
